overrides.override_summary: Post-term noncompete (FA Section 18.3) may not be enforceable under California law.
overrides.why_it_matters: California franchisees may be free to operate a competing business immediately after termination.

overrides.override_summary: Texas choice of law (FA Section 28.1) may not be enforceable under California law.
overrides.why_it_matters: California courts may apply California franchise law instead of Texas law.

overrides.override_summary: General release at renewal or transfer may be limited by CA Corporations Code Sections 31512 and Business & Professions Code Section 200010.
overrides.why_it_matters: Franchisee cannot waive rights under the California Franchise Investment Law or Franchise Relations Act.

overrides.override_summary: Maximum interest rate in California is 10%, overriding the 18% FA rate.
overrides.why_it_matters: Late payment interest capped at 10% instead of 18% for California franchisees.

overrides.override_summary: No disclaimer or questionnaire may waive claims of fraud in the inducement. Supersedes any inconsistent document term.
overrides.why_it_matters: Strong anti-fraud protection — franchisee retains right to rely on all representations made during sales process.

overrides.override_summary: Termination upon bankruptcy may not be enforceable under federal bankruptcy law. California Business and Professions Code Sections 20000-20043 provide rights concerning termination, transfer, or non-renewal.
overrides.why_it_matters: Additional termination protections beyond the FA terms.

overrides.override_summary: Must provide FDD at least 7 days prior to execution of binding agreement or payment, whichever occurs first.
overrides.why_it_matters: Shorter disclosure window than federal 14-day requirement.

overrides.override_summary: Unilateral termination prohibited unless material violation and not in bad faith (IC 23-2-2.7-1(7)).
overrides.why_it_matters: Franchisee gets good cause and good faith protection against termination.

overrides.override_summary: Franchisor cannot require prospective general release of claims subject to Indiana Deceptive Franchise Practices Act (IC 23-2-2.7-1(5)).
overrides.why_it_matters: Franchisee preserves right to bring claims under Indiana franchise law despite renewal/transfer releases.

overrides.override_summary: Franchisor may not accept rebates from persons with whom franchisee does business unless properly accounted for and submitted (IC 23-2-2.7-1(4)).
overrides.why_it_matters: Transparency and rebate pass-through protection.

overrides.override_summary: Indiana law applies to disputes covered by Indiana franchise laws. Indiana law supersedes conflicting FA provisions.
overrides.why_it_matters: Texas choice of law overridden for Indiana franchise law matters.

overrides.override_summary: Litigation regarding FA in Indiana; other litigation in franchisor's choice of law state.
overrides.why_it_matters: Indiana franchisees can litigate FA disputes in Indiana.

overrides.override_summary: Post-term covenant not to compete modified to comply with IC 23-2-2.7-1(9).
overrides.why_it_matters: Indiana may limit scope/duration of noncompete.

overrides.override_summary: General release at renewal or transfer does not release claims under Maryland Franchise Registration and Disclosure Law.
overrides.why_it_matters: Maryland franchise law claims are preserved despite general release.

overrides.override_summary: Franchisee may bring lawsuit in Maryland for claims under Maryland Franchise Registration and Disclosure Law.
overrides.why_it_matters: Maryland franchisees can litigate in Maryland despite Texas forum clause.

overrides.override_summary: Section 14-226 prohibits requiring assent to release, estoppel, or waiver of liability as condition of purchase.
overrides.why_it_matters: Anti-waiver protection at point of purchase.

overrides.override_summary: Minn. Stat. § 80C.21 and Rule 2860.4400(J) prohibit requiring litigation outside Minnesota.
overrides.why_it_matters: Minnesota franchisees cannot be forced to litigate in Texas.

overrides.override_summary: Any choice of law provision purporting to bind Minnesota resident or MN-organized entity to waive compliance with Minn. Stat. § 80C is void.
overrides.why_it_matters: Minnesota franchise law cannot be overridden by Texas choice of law.

overrides.override_summary: Franchisor must indemnify Minnesota franchisees against third-party trademark infringement claims (condition: immediate notice and tender of defense).
overrides.why_it_matters: Minnesota requires trademark indemnification that the base FA does not provide.

overrides.override_summary: Rights under Article 33 of NY General Business Law and regulations remain in force despite general release at renewal/transfer (GBL Sections 687.4, 687.5).
overrides.why_it_matters: NY franchise law claims preserved.

overrides.override_summary: Franchisee may terminate the agreement on any grounds available by law.
overrides.why_it_matters: Unlike the base FA which has no franchisee termination right, NY grants statutory termination rights.

overrides.override_summary: New York Franchise Law governs claims arising under that law, notwithstanding Texas choice of law.
overrides.why_it_matters: NY franchise law claims governed by NY law, not Texas.

overrides.override_summary: Franchisor may not assign FA rights except to assignee willing and able to assume obligations (added to FA Section 15).
overrides.why_it_matters: Protection against assignment to shell entity.

overrides.override_summary: Franchisee may cancel transaction within 5 business days after signing.
overrides.why_it_matters: Cooling-off period for Ohio franchisees.

overrides.override_summary: 90 days prior written notice of termination, cancellation, or substantial change. 60 days to cure (10 days for monetary). If cured, notice is void.
overrides.why_it_matters: Significantly longer notice/cure periods than the base FA (30/10 days).

overrides.override_summary: Wisconsin Fair Dealership Law (Ch. 135, Stats.) supersedes any inconsistent FA provisions.
overrides.why_it_matters: Wisconsin franchise law trumps conflicting FA terms.
